heroOverview: Epispadias is a rare congenital malformation in which the urethral opening forms on the dorsal (upper) surface of the penis in males, or is displaced anteriorly along the upper vaginal wall and clitoris in females, rather than at the anatomical tip. It occurs in approximately 1 in 117,000 live male births and 1 in 480,000 female births, making it one of the rarest congenital genitourinary anomalies. The defect arises from faulty closure of the urogenital sinus during weeks 4-7 of fetal development and falls within the exstrophy-epispadias complex — a spectrum that includes bladder exstrophy at its most severe end. The degree of anatomical abnormality determines the clinical presentation: a minor glanular epispadias may cause only mild voiding difficulty, whereas a complete epispadias, in which the urethra is a flat open plate extending from bladder neck to tip, is almost universally associated with urinary incontinence. Surgical reconstruction is the standard of care and has transformed outcomes over the past three decades.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 117,000 male births and 1 in 480,000 female births (data from European and US population registries)
keyStats.demographics: Male-to-female ratio approximately 4:1; isolated epispadias accounts for roughly 10% of the exstrophy-epispadias complex spectrum
keyStats.avgAge: Diagnosis typically at birth or early infancy on physical examination; prenatal detection is uncommon
keyStats.globalCases: Fewer than 1,000 new cases per year in the United States; global figures extrapolated from registry data suggest several thousand cases annually worldwide

definition: Epispadias (ICD-10: Q64.0) is a congenital anomaly characterised by incomplete tubularisation of the urethra, leaving the dorsal urethral wall open and the urethral meatus displaced to the dorsum of the penis in males or the anterior vaginal wall in females. It is the least severe manifestation of the exstrophy-epispadias complex, which includes cloacal exstrophy at one end and isolated glanular epispadias at the other. In males, epispadias is classified by the position of the urethral meatus: glanular (limited to the glans), penile shaft (most common isolated form, associated with penile dorsal curvature called dorsal chordee), and penopubic or subsymphyseal (extending to the base of the penis, virtually always with bladder neck incompetence and urinary incontinence). In females, the defect involves bifid clitoris, patulous urethral opening anteriorly, and wide pubic diastasis; urinary incontinence is nearly universal. The bladder in isolated epispadias is intact and open anteriorly, but the bladder neck may be incompetent depending on extent of the meatal displacement. The pubic symphysis is diverged in all but the mildest cases, contributing to the characteristic dorsal penile curvature and waddling gait. Management requires a paediatric urologist and, in complex cases, a multidisciplinary team including urology, psychology, and reconstructive surgery.

typesClassification.description: The mildest form; the meatus opens on the dorsal surface of the glans. The urethra is otherwise intact, bladder neck competence is preserved, and continence is usually maintained. Very uncommon.
The meatus lies on the dorsal penile shaft, accompanied by dorsal chordee and a bifid glans. Bladder neck function is variable; about 50% are continent. The most common isolated male form.

The most severe isolated form; the urethral plate extends from the bladder neck to the glans. Pubic diastasis is prominent, bladder neck is incompetent, and urinary incontinence is virtually universal without reconstruction.
The meatus opens above the bifid clitoris, accompanied by wide pubic diastasis and a patulous urethra. Urinary incontinence is nearly universal without surgical repair.

typesClassification.type: Epispadias as part of bladder exstrophy-epispadias complex
typesClassification.description: Epispadias accompanies bladder exstrophy in all cases; management then addresses both the open bladder plate and the urethral malformation in a staged reconstruction.

primarySymptoms: Urinary incontinence — continuous dribbling of urine from the wide open or incompetent urethra, present in virtually all penopubic and female cases from birth.
Abnormal meatal position — the urethral opening is visible on the dorsum of the penis or above the clitoris rather than at the expected location, apparent at birth.
Dorsal chordee — the penis is curved upward (dorsally) and appears foreshortened; the degree correlates with how proximal the meatal defect is.
Bifid glans — the glans is split into two lateral halves in males with shaft or penopubic epispadias, giving a characteristic flattened appearance.
Bifid clitoris — in females, the clitoris is divided into two parts flanking the widened urethral opening, a hallmark of female epispadias.
Wide pubic diastasis — the pubic bones are separated rather than fused at the symphysis; in severe cases this gives a characteristic broad-based stance and waddling gait.
Recurrent urinary tract infections — incomplete bladder emptying or pooled urine from an incompetent bladder neck increases infection risk from infancy.
Wetting with no sensation of urgency — children with incompetent bladder neck lack the normal sensation of a full bladder and wet continuously rather than in large bursts.
Umbilical or inguinal hernia — frequently co-occurs because pubic diastasis alters abdominal wall tension and the patent processus vaginalis remains open.
Reflux-related flank discomfort — vesicoureteral reflux complicates approximately 30-40% of epispadias cases; older children may describe recurrent back pain or infection.
earlyWarningSigns: Visible dorsal urethral opening on newborn examination — should prompt specialist referral before discharge.
Constant dampness of the nappy from birth without identifiable anorectal fistula.
Midline pubic gap palpable on neonatal examination.
A wide, flattened urethral groove noticed during nappy changes in the first weeks of life.
emergencySigns: Signs of urosepsis in a neonate or infant with known epispadias — high fever, pallor, poor feeding, tachycardia — require immediate hospital assessment.
Complete urinary retention after bladder neck reconstruction — rare, but signals anastomotic oedema or stricture requiring catheter decompression.
Acute haematuria following trauma to the urethral plate in an uncorrected infant.
Sudden worsening of continence after prior successful repair suggesting dehiscence of the bladder neck reconstruction.

causes.description: During weeks 4-7 of embryogenesis the cloacal membrane normally thins and ruptures in a controlled sequence. Failure of the infraumbilical mesoderm to migrate properly before membrane rupture leaves the dorsal urethra open; the more extensive the migration failure, the more proximal the meatal defect and the broader the spectrum toward bladder exstrophy.

The urethral plate normally rolls into a tube from posterior to anterior. In epispadias the dorsal fold fails to close, leaving a flat urethral plate open across the dorsum of the penis or vestibule; the defect is one of tubularisation failure rather than urethral absence.

The mesenchymal cells that normally bridge the pubic bones diverge in epispadias, producing a degree of pubic diastasis that widens in proportion to the severity of the meatal defect. This diastasis impairs the pelvic floor support needed for bladder neck competence.

No single gene has been identified, but copy number variants — particularly deletions at chromosome 22q11 and ISL1 gene variants — are overrepresented in exstrophy-epispadias registry cohorts. Twin studies show a concordance rate of approximately 68% in monozygotic pairs, confirming a genetic component alongside environmental triggers.

Some epidemiological studies have associated maternal smoking, first-trimester progesterone supplementation, and advanced paternal age with modestly increased exstrophy-epispadias complex risk, though effect sizes are small and causality unconfirmed.

riskFactors.description: Epispadias is approximately four times more common in males than females, reflecting the greater susceptibility of the male urogenital sinus to dorsal tubularisation failure.
The recurrence risk for siblings of an affected child is approximately 1 in 100 — roughly 400-fold the population risk. Offspring of an affected individual carry an estimated 1 in 70 risk.
Deletions at 22q11.2 and variants in ISL1 and TP63 have been found in registry cohorts, suggesting that congenital midline malformation predisposition genes contribute to risk.

Several case-control studies report a modest association between maternal first-trimester smoking and exstrophy-epispadias, possibly mediated by hypoxia-induced cloaca development disruption; risk magnitude is small.
Exstrophy-epispadias complex is reported at slightly higher frequency in singletons conceived by in vitro fertilisation versus natural conception, though the mechanism is not established.
Population-based registries in Europe and North America report higher incidence in white populations compared with African American or Asian populations, though universal reporting is incomplete.

affectedDemographics: Male neonates — the vast majority of affected individuals
Female neonates — less common but with high rates of complete incontinence
Infants with prenatal or neonatal diagnosis of bladder exstrophy (epispadias is a universal component)
Children of parents with a personal or family history of exstrophy-epispadias complex
Children with other midline congenital malformations such as spinal dysraphism or anorectal anomalies
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of epispadias is clinical in most cases, made on newborn physical examination when the dorsal urethral opening is identified. The position of the meatus on the dorsum of the penis or above the bifid clitoris is unmistakable. In females, the wide pubic diastasis and bifid clitoris are the primary clinical signs; the meatal position may be less immediately obvious. Neonatal assessment documents the level of the meatal defect (glanular, penile, or penopubic in males), the degree of pubic diastasis, bladder neck competence, the presence of dorsal chordee, and penile length and stretch. Renal-bladder ultrasound is performed in all cases to assess the upper tracts and identify associated hydronephrosis or renal anomalies. Voiding cystourethrogram is indicated when vesicoureteral reflux is suspected or prior to bladder neck reconstruction. Urodynamics — including cystometry and sphincter electromyography — are performed before planned bladder neck reconstruction to assess bladder capacity, compliance, and sphincter function. Prenatal diagnosis is uncommon for isolated epispadias; suspicion may arise from a prenatal ultrasound showing absence of a normal bladder filling pattern, but the definitive diagnosis is clinical at birth. Chromosomal microarray is offered when other anomalies co-occur. The key clinical distinction from hypospadias is the dorsal meatal position and associated dorsal curvature in epispadias, versus the ventral position and ventral chordee in hypospadias.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Primary diagnostic test — identifies dorsal meatal position, bifid glans or clitoris, pubic diastasis, and degree of chordee, classifying the severity and guiding urgent referral.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thorough neonatal genital examination by the paediatric team, usually completed in the delivery room or neonatal unit.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses upper tract anatomy, identifies hydronephrosis, measures bladder wall thickness and capacity, and detects associated renal anomalies.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive abdominal ultrasound with gel applied to the skin; takes 15-25 minutes; no sedation required in most newborns.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates vesicoureteral reflux (present in ~30-40% of epispadias cases) and defines the bladder neck anatomy before reconstruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A urethral catheter delivers contrast into the bladder under fluoroscopy; images are taken during bladder filling and voiding; mild catheter discomfort.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises bladder capacity, compliance, and sphincter function before bladder neck reconstruction to predict continence outcomes and optimise surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small catheter and pressure sensor are placed; the bladder is filled slowly with saline while pressure measurements are recorded; takes 30-60 minutes and may be uncomfortable.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies copy number variants or chromosomal deletions in children with additional congenital anomalies or a family history suggestive of a syndromic cause.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results available in 2-4 weeks; genetic counselling is offered alongside the test.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides detailed mapping of pelvic floor muscles and sphincter anatomy in complex cases, helping surgeons plan bladder neck reconstruction and predict functional outcomes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Requires sedation or general anaesthesia in young children; takes 30-45 minutes; no radiation.

treatmentOverview: Epispadias treatment is surgical and is tailored to the severity of the defect and the child's anatomy. The goals are to reconstruct a functional, normally placed urethra, achieve urinary continence, correct penile deformity to allow satisfactory sexual function in adulthood, and preserve upper tract function. For males with penopubic epispadias — the most common severe form — the standard approach is staged reconstruction. The first-stage Cantwell-Ransley or Mitchell-Bagli repair, typically performed at 6-12 months of age, reconstructs the urethra, straightens the penis by releasing dorsal chordee, and creates a neoglans. Bladder neck reconstruction — most commonly the Young-Dees-Leadbetter or Mitchell technique — is performed as a second stage, usually at 2-5 years of age when the child shows psychological readiness and the bladder has reached adequate capacity (typically at least 60-85 mL confirmed on urodynamics). Bladder augmentation with bowel segments may be needed when the bladder capacity is too small for successful neck reconstruction. For girls, genital reconstruction addresses the bifid clitoris and widened vaginal opening, and bladder neck repair follows. In penile or glanular epispadias with intact bladder neck competence, a single-stage urethroplasty may suffice. Approximately 70-80% of children achieve social continence (fewer than 1 pad per day) after bladder neck reconstruction, and outcomes continue to improve with technique refinements. Pubic symphysis closure — formal osteotomy or symphyseal closure — is used selectively when pelvic diastasis is significant, to improve sphincteric support. Long-term follow-up into adulthood addresses sexual function, fertility, and renal function monitoring.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotic prophylaxis
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose daily prophylaxis with trimethoprim or cephalexin is given from diagnosis until after definitive reconstruction to prevent febrile UTIs. Also covers the post-operative period while catheters are in situ.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces febrile UTI frequency by approximately 50-60% in congenital urinary anomaly prophylaxis studies; prevents upper tract scarring in the pre-operative period.

medicalTreatments.name: Anticholinergic therapy (oxybutynin)
medicalTreatments.description: Initiated in children with bladder overactivity detected on urodynamics; improves bladder compliance and capacity before bladder neck reconstruction, improving the likelihood of surgical continence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves clinically meaningful bladder capacity improvement in approximately 60-70% of children with small-capacity overactive bladders awaiting reconstruction.

medicalTreatments.name: Clean intermittent catheterisation (CIC)
medicalTreatments.description: Used post-operatively in children who void incompletely after bladder neck repair or following augmentation cystoplasty; maintains low bladder pressures and prevents upper tract deterioration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effectively protects upper tract function in virtually all augmented bladder patients who adhere to the regimen.

medicalTreatments.name: Artificial urinary sphincter (AUS)
medicalTreatments.description: An implantable cuff device placed around the bladder neck or proximal urethra provides mechanical continence when repeated bladder neck reconstructions have not achieved adequate closure. Used in older children or young adults after growth is established.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves social continence (fewer than 1 pad daily) in approximately 70-85% of epispadias patients in long-term series.

surgicalOptions.name: Cantwell-Ransley or Mitchell-Bagli urethroplasty
surgicalOptions.description: First-stage reconstruction of the urethral tube, penile straightening by releasing dorsal chordee, and glanular reconstruction; performed at 6-12 months of age.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Urethral reconstruction achieved in >90% of cases; fistula and stricture complication rates around 10-20% requiring minor revision

surgicalOptions.name: Young-Dees-Leadbetter bladder neck reconstruction
surgicalOptions.description: Tubularises the bladder neck and posterior urethra using native muscle to create a competent sphincteric mechanism; the most widely used continence procedure for epispadias.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Social continence achieved in approximately 70-80% of carefully selected patients at expert centres

surgicalOptions.name: Bladder augmentation cystoplasty
surgicalOptions.description: Bowel (most commonly ileum or colon) is used to enlarge a small-capacity bladder to sufficient volume for effective neck reconstruction; usually combined with clean intermittent catheterisation thereafter.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Bladder capacity reliably increased to adequate levels in >90% of augmented patients; combined continence rates with bladder neck repair approach 85-90%

surgicalOptions.name: Artificial urinary sphincter implantation
surgicalOptions.description: Mechanical sphincter device implanted around the bladder neck as salvage for repeated failed neck reconstructions; used in older children or adults.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Social continence in approximately 70-85% of epispadias/exstrophy patients in published series

alternativeTreatments.name: Pelvic floor physiotherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Targeted pelvic floor exercises are used as an adjunct after bladder neck reconstruction to maximise residual sphincteric function. Evidence in epispadias specifically is limited, but physiotherapy is widely incorporated into post-operative continence programmes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Biofeedback
alternativeTreatments.description: Electromyographic biofeedback of pelvic floor muscle activity helps some children learn to coordinate voiding and sphincter contraction; used as an adjunct in children with partial continence after reconstruction.

whySeeSpecialist: Epispadias requires a specialist paediatric urologist with specific experience in urethral reconstruction and bladder neck surgery, ideally at a centre that manages a significant volume of exstrophy-epispadias complex cases each year. Outcomes — particularly continence rates — are clearly better at high-volume centres. Long-term follow-up by paediatric urology, nephrology, and psychology is essential through childhood and the transition into adult urology care.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a paediatric urologist at a centre that performs at least 10-15 exstrophy-epispadias reconstructions per year. Confirm access to paediatric urodynamics, paediatric anaesthesia experienced with prolonged reconstructive procedures, and a multidisciplinary team including neonatology, nephrology, child psychology, and reconstructive surgery. Ask what proportion of their epispadias patients achieve social continence after bladder neck repair and whether they offer long-term transition to adult urology follow-up.
preventionStrategies: Genetic counselling is offered to families with an affected child before any future pregnancy, given the elevated recurrence risk.
Prenatal ultrasound may detect associated bladder exstrophy features, prompting planning for delivery at a centre with a paediatric urology team on standby.
Antibiotic prophylaxis from birth prevents UTIs and upper tract damage before surgical repair.
Surgical repair at the optimal age — 6-12 months for urethroplasty and 2-5 years for bladder neck reconstruction — preserves the best achievable long-term continence.
lifestyleModifications: Use age-appropriate absorbent products until continence is achieved post-operatively; plan school re-entry around the anticipated continence timeline.
Follow the catheterisation schedule precisely if clean intermittent catheterisation has been prescribed after augmentation; do not skip catheterisations.
Encourage adequate fluid intake to maintain dilute urine and reduce infection risk.
Engage with child psychology support early — body image and peer relationships are common concerns in affected children from school age onward.
Monitor blood pressure regularly, as renal scarring from repeated UTIs or upper tract obstruction can drive hypertension.

recommended: Adequate hydration to keep urine dilute and reduce infection risk
Fibre-rich foods to prevent constipation, which worsens bladder instability and can affect continence post-reconstruction
Balanced calcium and vitamin D intake, important if long-term antibiotic use or urinary diversion has altered gut absorption
Low-sodium diet if hypertension is present
avoid: Caffeine and carbonated drinks, which can exacerbate bladder overactivity
Excess salt if blood pressure is elevated
Artificial sweeteners and citrus in large amounts if they trigger bladder irritability post-operatively

exerciseGuidelines: After successful urethral reconstruction, age-appropriate physical activity is encouraged without restriction. Children with a solitary kidney or significant renal impairment are advised to avoid high-contact collision sports. Pelvic floor exercises are specifically encouraged from school age onward to support continence after bladder neck reconstruction. Participation in normal schooling and recreational activities is a core treatment goal.
dailyManagement: Administer antibiotic prophylaxis exactly as prescribed; do not stop without specialist guidance.
Perform clean intermittent catheterisation on the prescribed schedule if catheterisation has been recommended.
Report any fever promptly — fever in a child with a urinary anomaly requires same-day urine testing.
Keep a voiding diary during the continence assessment period before and after bladder neck reconstruction.
Attend all urodynamic and imaging follow-up appointments; silent upper tract deterioration can occur without symptoms.
Arrange psychological support or peer group connection for school-age children dealing with incontinence management.
prognosis: The long-term prognosis for children with epispadias managed at experienced centres has improved substantially over the past 30 years. Social continence — defined as needing fewer than 1 pad per day — is achieved in approximately 70-80% of patients after bladder neck reconstruction in published series from dedicated centres, with some contemporary series reporting rates above 80%. Sexual function in adulthood, while often affected by penile foreshortening and altered ejaculatory pathways, is preserved in the majority of males who undergo skilled reconstruction, and paternity via assisted reproduction has been reported. Renal function is maintained in most patients who are diagnosed early, treated promptly, and followed regularly. Psychological well-being, particularly regarding body image and relationships, is an area requiring sustained attention; peer support and psychological input from childhood onward improve long-term quality of life. Adults with epispadias require lifelong urological surveillance.
recoveryTimeline: Urethroplasty at 6-12 months involves 5-7 inpatient days with a catheter in situ for 3-4 weeks; full recovery takes 4-6 weeks. Bladder neck reconstruction requires 7-14 inpatient days; the catheter remains for 4-6 weeks post-operatively; school-age return takes 6-8 weeks. Bladder augmentation has a longer recovery of 2-3 weeks inpatient and 6-12 weeks to full activity. Continence outcomes become assessable 3-6 months after bladder neck repair.
complications: Urethrocutaneous fistula after urethroplasty — the most common post-operative complication, occurring in roughly 10-20% and usually requiring minor revision.
Urethral stricture — scarring narrows the reconstructed urethra, causing obstructive voiding symptoms and requiring dilatation or surgical revision.
Persistent urinary incontinence after bladder neck reconstruction — occurs in 20-30% of patients and may require further surgery, including artificial urinary sphincter implantation.
Vesicoureteral reflux — present in ~30-40% preoperatively; new-onset or worsening reflux can follow reconstruction and requires monitoring and re-intervention if significant.
Bladder augmentation complications — including mucus retention, recurrent UTIs, metabolic acidosis, and, rarely, augmentation-segment malignancy in long-term follow-up.
Psychological morbidity — body image disturbance, difficulty with intimate relationships, and depression are documented in epispadias cohorts and require proactive psychological support.

supportResources.name: Association for the Bladder Exstrophy Community (A-BE-C)
supportResources.description: International patient and family support organisation for those affected by the exstrophy-epispadias complex, offering peer connection and educational resources.

supportResources.name: Society for Pediatric Urology — Patient Education
supportResources.description: Patient resources from the leading North American paediatric urology professional body.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Bladder Control Problems in Children
supportResources.description: Comprehensive overview of urinary incontinence and management in children from the National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

sources.title: Gearhart JP, Baird AD. The bladder exstrophy-epispadias-cloacal exstrophy complex. Campbell-Walsh Urology, 10th ed., 2011
Kibar Y, Roth CC, Frimberger D, Kropp BP. Long-term results of the Pippi-Salle procedure for epispadias incontinence. J Urol 2009;181:1338-41
Nelson CP, Dunn RL, Wei JT. Contemporary epidemiology of bladder exstrophy in the United States. J Urol 2005;173:1728-31
Ebert AK, Reutter H, Ludwig M, Rösch WH. The exstrophy-epispadias complex. Orphanet J Rare Dis 2009;4:23
World Journal of Urology — Epispadias repair outcomes review: EAA/ESPU guidelines on hypospadias and related anomalies
NIDDK — Bladder Control Problems in Children (Urinary Incontinence)

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypospadias
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypospadias places the urethral meatus on the ventral (under) surface of the penis with ventral chordee, whereas epispadias places it on the dorsal (upper) surface with dorsal chordee — opposite anatomy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bladder exstrophy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bladder exstrophy involves complete eversion of the bladder through an absent anterior abdominal wall; epispadias is a urethral defect only, though all exstrophy cases also have epispadias.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cloacal exstrophy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cloacal exstrophy is the most severe complex, with exstrophied bladder halves flanking an exposed intestinal plate; epispadias is isolated to the urethra without bladder or intestinal wall involvement.

relatedConditions.name: Bladder exstrophy
relatedConditions.relevance: Epispadias is a core component of the exstrophy-epispadias complex; bladder exstrophy always includes epispadias and shares the same reconstructive surgical principles.

relatedConditions.name: Penile hypospadias
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypospadias is the ventral-meatal counterpart to epispadias; the two conditions are part of the spectrum of urethral malformations and both require staged urethral reconstruction.

relatedConditions.name: Vesicoureteral reflux
relatedConditions.relevance: VUR complicates roughly 30-40% of epispadias cases and must be addressed as part of upper tract management to prevent renal scarring.

relatedConditions.name: Prune belly syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Another rare congenital urological complex with abdominal wall and urinary tract involvement, included in the differential of complex neonatal urological presentations.

faqs.question: What is epispadias?
faqs.answer: Epispadias is a birth defect where the urethral opening forms on the upper surface of the penis in boys, or above the clitoris in girls, instead of at the normal location. It can cause urinary incontinence and abnormal genital appearance. Surgery corrects the urethra and, in most cases, restores continence.

faqs.question: How common is epispadias?
faqs.answer: Epispadias affects roughly 1 in 117,000 male births and 1 in 480,000 female births, making it one of the rarest congenital urological conditions. Boys are affected about four times more often than girls. Most cases are isolated, but the condition can occur as part of the broader exstrophy-epispadias complex.

faqs.question: What is the difference between epispadias and hypospadias?
faqs.answer: Epispadias places the urethral opening on the upper (dorsal) surface of the penis, causing the penis to curve upward and nearly always causing incontinence in severe forms. Hypospadias places the opening on the underside (ventral surface), with downward curvature. They are anatomically opposite defects requiring different surgical corrections.

faqs.question: Will my child be incontinent?
faqs.answer: Most children with penopubic (most severe) or female epispadias are incontinent from birth because the bladder neck does not close properly. After staged surgical reconstruction, approximately 70-80% of children achieve social continence — staying dry with fewer than one pad per day. Outcomes depend on the severity of the defect and the centre's experience.

faqs.question: When is surgery performed for epispadias?
faqs.answer: Urethral reconstruction is typically done at 6-12 months of age. Bladder neck reconstruction to achieve continence is a second stage, usually at 2-5 years when the bladder has grown large enough. The timing is individualised based on urodynamic findings and the child's readiness.

faqs.question: What surgery is used to fix epispadias?
faqs.answer: Surgery in two stages is standard for severe epispadias. The first stage — Cantwell-Ransley or Mitchell-Bagli repair — straightens the penis, closes the dorsal urethral plate into a tube, and reconstructs the glans. The second stage — bladder neck reconstruction, most often the Young-Dees-Leadbetter technique — creates a continence mechanism. Some children also need bladder enlargement with bowel tissue.

faqs.question: Is epispadias related to bladder exstrophy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Epispadias and bladder exstrophy are part of the same exstrophy-epispadias complex — a spectrum of related birth defects caused by the same embryological failure. All children with bladder exstrophy also have epispadias, but epispadias can occur on its own, without the open bladder wall of exstrophy.

faqs.question: Can epispadias be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Isolated epispadias is rarely detected on prenatal ultrasound. Bladder exstrophy-epispadias complex may be suspected when a normal filled bladder is not seen, but definitive diagnosis is made at birth on clinical examination. Epispadias alone does not produce the dramatic ultrasound findings seen with exstrophy.

faqs.question: Will epispadias affect my child's sexual development?
faqs.answer: With skilled reconstruction, most males retain erectile function and satisfactory penile appearance. Some experience retrograde ejaculation or reduced ejaculatory volume, and fertility may require assisted reproduction. Girls can achieve normal sexual function and may carry a pregnancy, though obstetric planning with a specialist is recommended. Psychological support is equally important alongside the physical repair.

faqs.question: Does epispadias run in families?
faqs.answer: There is a clear familial risk. Siblings of an affected child have roughly a 1 in 100 chance of having the same condition — about 400 times the general population risk. Children of an affected parent carry an estimated 1 in 70 risk. Genetic counselling is offered to all families after an epispadias diagnosis.

faqs.question: What is bladder neck reconstruction?
faqs.answer: Bladder neck reconstruction reshapes the tissue at the base of the bladder into a tube that can contract and hold urine. The Young-Dees-Leadbetter technique is the most widely used approach, and it achieves social continence in about 70-80% of children at expert centres. It is performed when urodynamic testing shows the bladder is large and compliant enough to hold urine safely.

faqs.question: What complications can occur after epispadias surgery?
faqs.answer: The most common early complication is a urethrocutaneous fistula — a small hole where urine leaks through the skin — occurring in roughly 10-20% of urethroplasties and usually needing minor repair. Longer-term complications include urethral stricture, persistent incontinence, and, if bladder augmentation was needed, mucus accumulation and metabolic changes requiring ongoing monitoring.

faqs.question: Does my child need antibiotics before surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Antibiotic prophylaxis — a low daily dose of trimethoprim or cephalexin — is given from diagnosis until surgical repair to prevent febrile urinary infections that could scar the kidneys. Prophylaxis also continues for several weeks after surgery while catheters are in situ.

faqs.question: What is clean intermittent catheterisation and will my child need it?
faqs.answer: Clean intermittent catheterisation (CIC) means passing a small lubricated tube into the bladder several times a day to empty it completely. It is needed after bladder augmentation cystoplasty because the enlarged bladder does not empty by itself. Not all children with epispadias require CIC — only those who need augmentation as part of their reconstruction.

faqs.question: What is an artificial urinary sphincter?
faqs.answer: An artificial urinary sphincter is a surgically implanted cuff that squeezes the urethra closed until the patient deliberately opens it with a pump in the scrotum or labia. It is used as a salvage procedure when repeated bladder neck repairs have not achieved continence. It achieves dryness in approximately 70-85% of epispadias patients in published series.

faqs.question: Will epispadias repair affect kidney function?
faqs.answer: Epispadias itself can cause vesicoureteral reflux and bladder dysfunction that put the kidneys at risk over time. With prompt surgery, antibiotic prophylaxis, and regular imaging and urodynamic follow-up, renal function is preserved in the great majority of children. Those with significant pre-operative upper tract damage carry a higher risk of hypertension and chronic kidney disease in adulthood.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take after epispadias surgery?
faqs.answer: Urethroplasty at 6-12 months involves about 5-7 hospital days with a catheter for 3-4 weeks and full recovery in 4-6 weeks. Bladder neck reconstruction requires 7-14 hospital days, a catheter for 4-6 weeks, and return to school in 6-8 weeks. Bladder augmentation has a longer recovery of 2-3 weeks inpatient and up to 12 weeks to full activity.

faqs.question: Are there support groups for epispadias?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Association for the Bladder Exstrophy Community (A-BE-C) at bladderexstrophy.com is an international organisation supporting families affected by the exstrophy-epispadias complex, offering peer support, educational events, and connections to experienced specialists. Online communities and paediatric urology centres often maintain contact lists of families willing to share their experiences.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed long term?
faqs.answer: Children require renal ultrasound and urodynamic assessment at regular intervals — typically annually until the urinary tract is confirmed stable. Blood pressure monitoring, renal function blood tests, and continence review continue into adulthood. Transition to an adult urology service should be planned proactively by mid-adolescence to ensure continuity of specialist care.

faqs.question: Can girls with epispadias have children?
faqs.answer: With appropriate reconstruction of the pelvic floor and urethra, many women with epispadias can become pregnant. The pelvic anatomy and any prior augmentation surgery affect mode of delivery, so obstetric planning with a maternal-fetal medicine specialist and urologist is recommended from early in any pregnancy.

metaDescription: Epispadias is a rare birth defect where the urethra opens on the upper penis or above the clitoris, causing incontinence. Learn diagnosis, surgery, and outcomes for children.
